title: React Suspense and Streaming

learningObjectives.text: Explain suspense boundaries using a new example and justify the result.
Explain streaming ui using a new example and justify the result.
Apply loading states using a new example and justify the result.

whyThisMatters: This topic turns react suspense and streaming from isolated terminology into a practical tool for reading, writing, and reviewing code. It belongs to the **Streaming, Rendering Strategies, and Caching** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Suspense boundaries** → **Streaming UI** → **Loading states**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: Suspense boundaries
sections.body: A Suspense boundary defines where fallback UI can appear while a child waits.

sections.title: Streaming UI
sections.body: Streaming sends ready parts of server-rendered UI before slower parts finish.

sections.title: Loading states
sections.body: Loading UI should preserve layout, identify what is pending, and avoid blocking unrelated content.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.explanation: The shell can stream while a slower region resolves.

commonMistakes: Marking a large route tree as client code when only a small interaction needs it.
Confusing React Server Components with server-side rendering.
Assuming cached data is fresh without an explicit policy and invalidation path.
Placing security-sensitive validation only in the client or middleware layer.
summary: React Suspense and Streaming connects Suspense boundaries, Streaming UI, Loading states.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

questions.prompt: A learner must use React Suspense and Streaming in a new situation. Which approach best demonstrates transferable understanding?

choices.label: Skip the underlying model and rely on memorized syntax, even when the result is unclear.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of React Suspense and Streaming.

choices.label: Treat the concept as isolated trivia and avoid connecting it to earlier prerequisites.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of React Suspense and Streaming.

choices.label: Apply Suspense boundaries deliberately, make the relevant inputs and assumptions explicit, and verify the observable result before adding more complexity.
choices.feedback: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.

choices.label: Add more state and abstraction immediately, before identifying the smallest requirement.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of React Suspense and Streaming.

questions.explanation: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.
questions.hint: Choose the option that would still work when the surrounding example changes.

questions.prompt: Which sequence is most reliable when solving a problem involving React Suspense and Streaming?

choices.label: Optimize first, then decide what the code is supposed to do.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of React Suspense and Streaming.

choices.label: Copy an unrelated implementation, hide the assumptions, and test only the happy path.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of React Suspense and Streaming.

choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from Suspense boundaries, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.
choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.

choices.label: Start with a framework abstraction and avoid checking the underlying language behavior.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of React Suspense and Streaming.

questions.explanation: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
questions.hint: Prefer a sequence that makes failures visible early.

questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best judgment about when to use React Suspense and Streaming?

choices.label: Choose based only on line count, without considering readability, correctness, or future change.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of React Suspense and Streaming.

choices.label: Avoid React Suspense and Streaming completely because all abstractions reduce maintainability.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of React Suspense and Streaming.

choices.label: Use React Suspense and Streaming when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.
choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.

choices.label: Use React Suspense and Streaming in every file because more abstraction is always better.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of React Suspense and Streaming.

questions.explanation: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.
